narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and plans to attend full-time, you've put real effort into your law school application, and we appreciate the dedication it took to get here. Your undergraduate GPA sits at 2.90, which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, while your family comes from a middle-income bracket, right in line with many others. In your school's grading, you ranked in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades and the 2nd decile for Year 3—both notably lower than the 6th and 5th decile averages seen among past applicants. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 61.5% chance that you would fail the bar, tipping the scales toward rejection to ensure we select students most likely to thrive in legal practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as lower performance there strongly signals challenges ahead compared to peers who ranked higher. Your Year 1 decile also contributed notably in the same direction, standing out as weaker than most. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 37—slightly above the 36.7 average—pushed meaningfully toward predicting bar passage success, showing your potential in that area. Your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance too, though not as strongly, while your middle-income background nudged slightly toward higher risk, unlike higher-earning families in the dataset. Being full-time, like over 90% of students, provided a small positive influence. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having competitive test scores but grades that lag behind the pack, which outweighed the positives here. To move forward, consider retaking courses or boosting your GPA through post-bac programs, prepping intensely for the bar early, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your LSAT strength. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage reapplying after addressing these academic gaps. Keep pushing; your story isn't over.
